TIER 1 (build it, it's the anchor): a daily unattended job using the official YouTube Data API v3 with one API key, walking channels.list -> uploads-playlist -> playlistItems.list -> videos.list to capture each local competitor's new uploads with titles, descriptions, publish dates and view/like/comment counts. Resolve channel IDs once and store them; never use search.list in the daily loop; you'll consume a few hundred of the free 10,000 daily units even with dozens of channels. This is clean, sanctioned, key-only, and Brazil-friendly. TIER 2 (transcripts — paid/optional, not official): the official captions endpoint is owner-only and useless for competitors, and self-hosted timedtext scraping breaks under YouTube's 2025-26 datacenter-IP/PoToken bot defenses. So either lean on title+description+tags (officially available) for text analysis, or pay a third-party transcript API (Supadata/TranscriptAPI/SerpApi, ideally with AI fallback) per video and keep it as an isolated enrichment step so its flakiness never takes down the metadata feed. Realistic path: ship Tier 1 now; treat Tier 2 as a budgeted add-on with graceful 'transcript unavailable' handling.
result.brazilCaveats: No Brazil-specific obstacle on the official metadata path: the YouTube Data API v3 is free, globally available, not geo-gated on the caller, and returns public competitor data regardless of where the job runs. A Google Cloud project can be created from Brazil with no billing needed for this quota tier. The only geo-flavored issue is on the unofficial transcript-scraping side, and even there the discriminator is datacenter-IP-vs-residential-IP reputation, not country — Brazilian cloud IPs get blocked by YouTube's bot defenses the same way AWS/GCP/Azure IPs do. That is one more reason to push transcripts onto a paid provider (which manages residential proxies) rather than self-hosting scraping on a Brazilian VPS. Higher-quota requests require Google's YouTube API Services compliance audit (English-language form, app review) but a single venue won't need it.

result.dimension: Monitoring competitors' ORGANIC short-form video (Instagram Reels, TikTok) + "what's trending in the wedding niche" — feasibility of an unattended daily routine, from Brazil, 2026
result.summary: An unattended daily routine is feasible, but only as a hybrid: ONE narrow official path on Instagram (the Business Discovery + Hashtag Search endpoints) plus paid third-party scrapers for everything else. There is NO official API that lets you read a TikTok competitor's public videos for commercial use, and Instagram's official "competitor" coverage is thin (numbers + caption text on Business/Creator accounts only). Crucially, no official API hands you the actual video file, so "have an AI watch the Reel" requires a scraper to grab the media URL first; the model then analyzes caption+cover+frames+audio transcript, not a true human-grade "watch". The realistic build is: Instagram Graph (Business Discovery) for your own-account-authenticated competitor numbers, Apify/EnsembleData-class scrapers (logged-out public data, ~US$1.70–75/mo+) for Reels/TikTok captions+metrics+video URLs, then Gemini-class multimodal analysis to summarize and a separate noise-vs-signal heuristic layer. The "tell me what's noise vs real" judgment is the part automation does worst — it can cluster and summarize, but ranking what actually matters in the wedding niche stays a human-in-the-loop or heavily-tuned step. Brazil adds no API access (everything is web/scraper or US-style commercial APIs); the one Brazil-specific official door — TikTok Research API for Brazilian researchers — is gated to youth-safety academic/non-profit use and excludes a commercial venue.

keyFindings.claim: Instagram has ONE genuine official path to competitor public data: the Business Discovery endpoint. It returns another professional account's followers_count, media_count, biography, website, and recent media with caption, like_count, comments_count and view_count (organic + paid) — without that account authorizing your app.
keyFindings.evidence: Meta's own docs: Business Discovery 'obtains metadata and basic metrics about other Instagram professional accounts'; media metrics include comments count, like count, view count. Multiple 2026 guides confirm you can poll it to track competitor follower growth and recent posts.
keyFindings.implication: This is the spine of a daily routine for KNOWN local competitors on Instagram: a cron job polls each competitor's user ID once/day and logs numbers + new captions. It is the only piece that is officially sanctioned rather than scraped.

verification.claim: Instagram has ONE genuine official path to competitor public data: the Business Discovery endpoint. It returns another professional account's followers_count, media_count, biography, website, and recent media with caption, like_count, comments_count and view_count (organic + paid) — without that account authorizing your app.
verification.correctedClaim: Confirmed and accurate on every load-bearing point — I tried to refute it and could not. business_discovery is indeed the ONLY official Meta endpoint that returns another Instagram professional account's public data without that account authorizing your app. It returns account fields (biography, name, username, website, profile_picture_url, followers_count, follows_count, media_count, id, ig_id) and a media edge with caption, comments_count, like_count, view_count, media_type, media_url, permalink, timestamp, children. Meta's own page states verbatim that "view_count includes paid and organic metrics," so the "(organic + paid)" parenthetical is exactly right. ONE precision the claim omits — decisive for an unattended routine: business_discovery is available ONLY under the "Instagram API with Facebook Login" path, NOT the newer "Instagram API with Instagram Login" Meta now defaults to. So the automation needs a Facebook app + a Facebook Page linked to YOUR own IG professional account, a long-lived Instagram User Access Token via Facebook Login, the permissions instagram_basic + instagram_manage_insights + pages_read_engagement (plus App Review), and lives within a ~200 calls/hour/user rate limit. Honest hard limits: it gives only a CURRENT snapshot — no follower/following lists, no audience demographics, no competitor-account insights, no stories, and no history; any time-series (e.g., daily follower growth) must be built by polling daily and storing it yourself. Critically for this project's goal, this endpoint is for ORGANIC content only — it is NOT the path to competitor PAID ADS (that is the separate Meta Ad Library); the view_count "paid+organic" wording refers to views on an organic post that was also boosted, not ad-library data. Recency check (2025-2026): the Jan 8, 2025 / Graph API v21 metric deprecations (profile_views, website_clicks, non-Reels video_views, etc.) hit OWN-account Insights, not business_discovery fields; Basic Display API EOL (Dec 4, 2024) does not affect it; business_discovery remains operational in 2026 (v22.0).
verification.evidence: Meta's canonical Business Discovery doc (under the Facebook-Login tree) confirms the no-authorization access, the account/media field lists, and states literally "Observe que view_count inclui métricas pagas e orgânicas" (view_count includes paid and organic metrics). The endpoint URL itself — /instagram-platform/instagram-api-with-facebook-login/business-discovery/ — proves it is Facebook-Login-only; the Instagram-Login API docs list its supported features (comment moderation, content publishing, media insights, messaging) and do NOT include business_discovery. The IG-User reference confirms required permissions (instagram_basic, instagram_manage_insights, pages_read_engagement) and the age-restricted exclusion. A 2026 scraping review and the April-2026 API reference gist independently confirm it is "the only official endpoint that supports competitor monitoring," returns data "without their OAuth approval," requires a linked Facebook Page, has a ~200 calls/hour limit, and "will never give you full follower lists or historical post insights on competitor accounts." Deprecation context (Jan 8 2025, v21; Basic Display EOL Dec 4 2024) confirmed via Meta changelog summaries — those affect own-account insights, not business_discovery.

keyFindings.claim: Business Discovery's limits make it a thin instrument, not a full monitor: it only works on Business/Creator accounts (not personal), must be queried by numeric user ID (not @username), age-restricted accounts return nothing, and it does NOT grant access to the media files themselves ('GET requests on any returned Instagram media will fail').
keyFindings.evidence: Meta docs: 'This does not grant permission to directly access the media objects... GET requests on any returned Instagram media will fail due to insufficient permissions'; 'Data about Instagram professional accounts with age restrictions will not be returned'; queries require the IG user ID e.g. 17841401441775531.
keyFindings.implication: You get the caption text and the numbers, but to actually let an AI SEE the Reel you must fetch the video by another means (scraper). For a venue whose competitors are all Business accounts this still covers most of them — but it is text+metrics intelligence, not visual.

verification.claim: Business Discovery's limits make it a thin instrument, not a full monitor: it only works on Business/Creator accounts (not personal), must be queried by numeric user ID (not @username), age-restricted accounts return nothing, and it does NOT grant access to the media files themselves ('GET requests on any returned Instagram media will fail').
verification.correctedClaim: Business Discovery is a thin instrument, not a full monitor. Three of the four limits hold exactly: (1) the TARGET must be an Instagram Business or Creator (professional) account — personal accounts return nothing; (2) age-restricted professional accounts return no data ("Data about Instagram professional accounts with age restrictions will not be returned"); (3) it does NOT grant access to the underlying media objects — Meta states "this does not grant permission to directly access the media objects," so a standalone GET on a returned media ID fails with insufficient permissions (you can read only the public fields nested inside the business_discovery response itself). The fourth point is BACKWARDS and is the imprecision: the competitor is looked up BY @username, not by numeric user ID — the call is GET /{YOUR-numeric-IG-user-id}?fields=business_discovery.username({COMPETITOR_USERNAME}). The numeric ID (e.g. 17841401441775531) is YOUR OWN account's ID, used as the path node you query against; the @username is how you name the competitor. So @username is required, not forbidden. Two further real constraints the claim omits: the CALLER must also have an IG Business/Creator account linked to a Facebook Page (plus a reviewed app with instagram_basic + a Page), and Business Discovery has its own separate rate limit — together reinforcing "thin instrument, not full monitor."
verification.evidence: Live official Meta Business Discovery doc, Limitations section, verbatim: "Não serão retornados dados sobre contas profissionais do Instagram com restrição de idade" (= "Data about age-restricted professional Instagram accounts will not be returned") — confirms age-restriction limit. Same doc, "basic media metrics" section, verbatim: "Observe que isso não concede permissão para acessar diretamente os objetos de mídia" (= "Note that this does not grant permission to directly access the media objects") — confirms media-access limit; the English string "GET requests on any returned Instagram media will fail due to insufficient permissions" is corroborated across multiple independent sources (keyapi.ai, Meta legacy /docs/instagram-api/business-discovery). The ig-user reference page confirms business/creator-only: "Retorna dados sobre outros usuários do Instagram com uma conta empresarial ou de criador de conteúdo." On the ID-vs-username point, the documented call shape is GET /{IG_USER_ID}?fields=business_discovery.username(<USERNAME>): the path node is the caller's numeric IG user ID (Meta example 17841405309211844), while the TARGET is named by @username — so the claim's "must be queried by numeric user ID (not @username)" inverts how the target is identified. Independent reference (jameschapman2c gist) states plainly: "You pass the target account's username as a parameter, not a numeric ID," and "Your app user must also have a Professional account." For the VVLEADHUB use case (daily unattended monitor of local wedding-venue competitors): Business Discovery yields only public profile counts and the public fields of recent media nested in the response — no media file download, no ads, no stories/reels analytics, no age-restricted accounts — so it is a thin profile-stats probe, not a content monitor, matching the claim's thesis.

keyFindings.claim: Using Business Discovery requires YOUR OWN Instagram Business/Creator account linked to a Facebook Page, an app, and Meta App Review for the instagram_basic / business permissions — it is your-account-authenticated access used to look outward, not a public open API.
keyFindings.evidence: 2026 guides: 'All Instagram API access in 2026 goes through the Instagram Graph API, which requires a Business or Creator account connected to a Facebook Page'; access needs scopes 'approved by Meta'. Note: own-account insights (reach, demographics) are own-account only.
keyFindings.implication: The venue (Vale Verde) needs to stand up and maintain a Meta app + App Review; the routine then runs under that app's token. This is a real setup/maintenance cost and a single point of failure (token expiry, policy changes) for the unattended job.

keyFindings.claim: For the 'what's trending in the wedding niche' goal, Instagram's official Hashtag Search endpoint (ig_hashtag_search -> recent_media / top_media) still exists and is NOT deprecated, returning top + recent public posts for a hashtag (id, media_type, media_url, caption, timestamp).
keyFindings.evidence: 2026 reference: 'Hashtag Search API status is not deprecated with no announced shutdown date'; supports top and recent public posts per hashtag. BUT hard cap: '30 unique hashtags per Instagram user per rolling 7 days' and no general keyword/location search — hashtags only.
keyFindings.implication: You can officially sample #casamento / #casamentonocampo etc. for trend discovery, but the 30-hashtags-per-7-days ceiling means you must pick a small fixed hashtag set and rotate carefully — it is a sampling probe, not a firehose. Personal-account viral posts may be missed.

keyFindings.claim: There is NO official commercial API to read a TikTok competitor's public videos. TikTok's Research API is free but eligibility is restricted to academic/non-profit researchers and 'commercial users, creators, and advertisers are explicitly ineligible'.
keyFindings.evidence: 2026 sources: Research API gives public videos/comments/user info but is limited to qualifying academic institutions (US/EEA/UK/CH), EU non-profits, and 'Brazilian academic or non-profit researchers studying youth safety'. 'If your company makes money and you want to use this for competitive analysis, expect a rejection.' The Commercial Content API is ad-transparency/regulatory, not general competitor monitoring.
keyFindings.implication: Any TikTok competitor monitoring for the venue MUST go through third-party scrapers/data APIs — there is no sanctioned path. This is a structural fact to design around, not a gap to wait out.

keyFindings.claim: Meta's research equivalent (Meta Content Library / API), which DOES include Instagram public posts and near-real-time search, is also academic/non-profit only — explicitly not for commercial purposes, and from Jan 2026 its compute enclave is paid.
keyFindings.evidence: Meta Transparency Center + Bellingcat: covers public Instagram posts from Creator/Business + high-follower personal accounts, but 'restricted to academic and non-profit use—not commercial purposes'; SOMAR free compute ended 2025-12-31, charging begins Jan 2026.
keyFindings.implication: Rules out the most powerful official cross-account search tool for a commercial venue. Reinforces that the only official commercial lever is Business Discovery + Hashtag Search; broad discovery needs scrapers or paid listening tools.

keyFindings.claim: In practice, competitor video monitoring at scale is done with scraper 'actors' / data APIs operating on LOGGED-OUT public data: Apify Instagram/TikTok scrapers (~US$1.70 per 1,000 results), EnsembleData (~US$75/mo+), TikAPI (~US$49/mo), Netrows (~€49/mo), TikHub (pay-per-request). These return caption, hashtags, like/comment/view counts AND the media CDN/video URL, with no personal login.
keyFindings.evidence: 2026 listings: Apify IG scraper returns captions, hashtags (arrays), like/comment counts, media CDN URLs, timestamps, 'no personal account needed... publicly visible, logged-out data'. Netrows/TikAPI/EnsembleData expose TikTok profiles, videos with engagement, captions, hashtags on public data without user login; RapidAPI providers warned as unstable ('providers frequently go offline').
keyFindings.implication: This is what actually powers an unattended routine for Reels + TikTok video content, INCLUDING getting the downloadable video URL the official APIs withhold. Budget is modest, but reliability is the risk: Instagram 'throttles aggressively', comment scraping is capped, and actors break when platforms change — the job needs retry/alerting and occasional maintenance.

keyFindings.claim: An AI CAN 'watch' a Reel/TikTok — multimodal models (e.g. Gemini Omni, announced Google I/O 2026-05-19) ingest video and analyze visual + audio + speech, transcribe dialogue, and flag culturally specific references — BUT only once you supply the actual video file/URL, which only the scraper layer provides.
keyFindings.evidence: 2026 sources: Gemini Omni 'unifies text, image, audio and video understanding'; video analyzers 'process public video URLs from YouTube, Vimeo, TikTok, Instagram, X... including Reels and Shorts', running multimodal processing across visual/audio/text layers into structured output.
keyFindings.implication: Technically you CAN go beyond caption+cover to true frame+audio analysis — the pipeline is scraper grabs MP4 -> multimodal model summarizes scene, style, music, claims. So 'at best read caption + cover' UNDER-states it, but it depends entirely on the scraper succeeding and on per-video model cost, which makes daily full-video analysis of many competitors expensive; caption+cover+transcript is the cheap default tier.

keyFindings.claim: Logged-out public scraping is the most legally defensible mode but still breaches platform ToS; GDPR/LGPD-style rules govern storage regardless of collection legality.
keyFindings.evidence: 2026 legal primers: Jan 2024 N.D. Cal. ruling (Bright Data v. Meta) held Meta's terms 'do not bar logged-off scraping of public data, nor the sale of that data'; Meta dropped the case — 'leading US contract-law authority for logged-out public scraping'. Yet 'Instagram, LinkedIn, and TikTok explicitly ban scraping in their terms', and GDPR/CCPA 'apply to how you store and use the data regardless of collection legality'. Apify itself is legal; 'your targets, fields, and method decide the risk'.
keyFindings.implication: Design the routine logged-out, store only what's needed (public business posts/metrics, not personal user PII), and treat the ToS-breach risk as a business decision. For a Brazilian operator, LGPD is the analog of GDPR for any personal data captured (commenter handles etc.) — minimize and avoid reselling.

keyFindings.claim: Off-the-shelf social listening suites (Brandwatch, Sprout Social, Talkwalker, Hootsuite) can monitor Instagram/TikTok conversations and trends turnkey, but they are enterprise-priced, generic, and the search evidence does NOT confirm wedding-niche depth or first-class Brazilian-Portuguese support.
keyFindings.evidence: 2026 comparisons: Brandwatch/Talkwalker 'track 30+ social networks plus millions of web pages'; Brandwatch's TikTok proposition is 'built around official partner access' (content/brand-mentions APIs). But sources 'did not contain specific information about Portuguese-language support in Brazil or niche wedding market applications'.
keyFindings.implication: These tools answer 'what's being said / trending' with less engineering, but cost and generality make them a poor fit for a small premium venue wanting deep, named-competitor video intelligence in pt-BR. Likely a supplement for broad trend sensing, not the core of the routine.

keyFindings.claim: The 'tell me what's noise vs what's real' goal is the weakest link for automation: pipelines can ingest, transcribe, cluster and summarize at scale, but ranking what genuinely matters in a specific premium-wedding niche is judgment the tooling does not reliably deliver unattended.
keyFindings.evidence: Multimodal tooling produces 'structured output you can reuse across research, reporting, and production workflows' and clusters trends, but the surveyed tools describe extraction/summarization, not validated niche-relevance ranking; social listening tools surface volume/sentiment, not curated significance.
keyFindings.implication: Realistically the daily routine should AUTOMATE collection + transcription + summarization + a tunable scoring heuristic (engagement velocity, novelty vs your own baseline, recurrence across competitors), then SURFACE a short ranked digest for a human to confirm. Expect to iterate the noise filter; 'fully unattended, trustworthy signal-vs-noise' is not a solved capability you can buy.

result.brazilCaveats: No official path improves from Brazil and one specifically closes. (1) Instagram Business Discovery + Hashtag Search are global and usable from Brazil under your own Meta app — unaffected by geography, fully applicable to local interior-SP competitors. (2) TikTok Research API has a Brazil-specific eligibility (Brazilian academic/non-profit researchers) but ONLY for youth-safety research — a commercial wedding venue does not qualify, so it is a dead end. (3) Meta Content Library is academic/non-profit only worldwide, paid compute from Jan 2026 — not for a commercial operator. (4) Third-party scraper APIs (Apify, EnsembleData, TikAPI, Netrows) are US/EU-based but operate on public data callable from anywhere, so they work from Brazil; pricing is in US$/€. (5) Social listening suites' Brazilian-Portuguese NLP quality for a narrow pt-BR wedding niche is unverified in the sources — validate sentiment/trend accuracy in pt-BR before relying on it. (6) LGPD (Brazil's GDPR analog) governs any personal data the routine captures (commenter handles, profile data) — keep the routine to public business content/metrics, minimize PII, do not resell; this dovetails with the repo's deferred-LGPD posture (keep hooks, do not over-collect).
result.bottomLine: Feasible as a HYBRID daily routine, not a single clean API. Realistic architecture: (A) Official spine — a Meta app under Vale Verde's own Instagram Business account polls Business Discovery once/day for each known local competitor (followers, media_count, new posts' captions + like/comment/view counts) and samples a fixed set of wedding hashtags via Hashtag Search (mind the 30-hashtags/7-days cap). (B) Scraper layer — Apify/EnsembleData-class actors on logged-out public data fetch Reels and TikTok posts INCLUDING the video URL the official APIs withhold, for ~US$ tens/month, with retry + failure alerting because actors break and platforms throttle. (C) Analysis layer — a multimodal model (Gemini-class) transcribes/analyzes caption+cover (cheap default) or full video frames+audio (expensive, reserve for flagged items), producing structured summaries. (D) Signal layer — a tunable scoring heuristic (engagement velocity, novelty vs your baseline, cross-competitor recurrence) ranks items into a short daily digest. Be brutally honest with the founder on three things: there is NO official TikTok competitor API (scraping is the only route, ToS-breach risk is real though logged-out is the defensible posture per Bright Data v. Meta); Instagram's official competitor coverage is text+numbers only (no media file, Business/Creator accounts only); and the 'what's noise vs real' verdict cannot be fully outsourced to tooling — automate collection/summarization, keep a human confirming the ranked digest until the heuristic earns trust. This should enter the repo as a discovery item then a scoped work-order under the deferred-LGPD discipline, not as ad-hoc scraping.

result.dimension: Paid third-party ad-intelligence & social-listening platforms for daily automated monitoring of named local competitors (paid ads + organic), from Brazil
result.summary:
For a daily UNATTENDED routine that pulls a NAMED local competitor's paid ads and organic posts, the pragmatic winner is NOT a turnkey ad-spy SaaS — it's Apify's web-UI scraper actors driven by their REST API. Apify's Facebook Ad Library Scraper takes a competitor's Facebook Page URL/ID, filters by country=BR, returns active commercial ad creatives/copy/CTAs/start-dates at ~$0.75/1,000 ads, and is fully schedulable via REST API; pair it with Apify's Instagram Profile Scraper (~$0.50–1.00/1,000 posts) for organic content. The reason you can't just use the OFFICIAL Meta Ad Library API: outside the EU, that API's archive returns only political/social-issue ads — ordinary wedding-venue commercial ads appear in the Ad Library WEB UI but are NOT reliably returned by the API for Brazil. So the official-API route is largely a dead end for this use case; the official, sanctioned alternative is to scrape the public Ad Library UI (what Apify does). Purpose-built ad-spy SaaS (AdSpy, BigSpy, Minea, PiPiADS) are index-based and ecommerce/dropshipping-oriented: a small Brazilian local wedding venue very likely is NOT in their database, and they have essentially no automation API — wrong tool. Foreplay Spyder is the best turnkey named-brand tracker (24/7 auto-tracking of a brand's Meta ads, historical record, alerts) and its API is in beta on higher tiers — the best "buy not build" option if you accept a per-seat SaaS. Social-listening suites (Brandwatch, Sprout, Brand24, Mention) are keyword/mention-oriented and expensive ($200–$5,000+/mo); they are a poor and overpriced fit for "fetch this one named account's own posts daily" vs. an Apify Instagram scraper.</parameter>

keyFindings.claim: The OFFICIAL Meta Ad Library API does NOT reliably return ordinary commercial (non-political) ads for Brazil; outside the EU its archive is restricted to political/social-issue ads (plus US housing/employment/credit). Commercial wedding-venue ads are visible in the Ad Library WEB UI but not in the API.
keyFindings.evidence: Multiple 2026 sources: the /ads_archive Graph API is 'strictly a subset: political and social-issue ads, plus Housing/Employment/Credit special categories in the US, all with seven-year retention'; 'Commercial ads are visible in the browser UI but are not returned by the API even with ad_type=ALL'; full all-ads transparency exists only in the EU under the DSA, and Brazil has no equivalent law. Note one source claims commercial creative IS exposed via API with limited fields — the safe operational read is: do not depend on the API for Brazilian commercial ads.
keyFindings.implication: The cheap, sanctioned, fully-automatable official API is effectively unavailable for this use case in Brazil. The daily routine must read the public Ad Library WEB UI (directly or via a scraper service), not the Graph API. Budget for scraping, not for a free API token.

verification.claim: The OFFICIAL Meta Ad Library API does NOT reliably return ordinary commercial (non-political) ads for Brazil; outside the EU its archive is restricted to political/social-issue ads (plus US housing/employment/credit). Commercial wedding-venue ads are visible in the Ad Library WEB UI but not in the API.
verification.correctedClaim: CONFIRMED, with two precision refinements. The official Meta Ad Library API (/ads_archive Graph endpoint) does NOT reliably return ordinary commercial (non-political) ads delivered in Brazil. Its archive scope is: (1) ads about social issues, elections or politics — globally, with ~7-year retention; PLUS (2) ALL ads (including commercial) that deliver to the EU and EU "associated territories" — a DSA-driven regime with ~1-year retention, beneficiary/payer, reach and targeting fields. Brazil is NOT in the EU/associated-territories set and has no DSA-equivalent commercial-ad-transparency law in force (PL 2630 / "Fake News Bill" is inactive as of late 2024; the 2025 ECA Digital, Lei 15.211/2025, governs minors online, not ad transparency). Therefore a wedding-venue's ordinary commercial ads are visible in the Ad Library WEB UI but are not returned by the API for Brazil even with ad_type=ALL — because ad_type=ALL means "all within the political+issue+EU-DSA scope," not "every ad." For an unattended Brazilian competitor-ad routine, the operational read in the original claim is correct: do NOT depend on /ads_archive for Brazilian commercial ads. PRECISION FIXES: (a) The 'US housing/employment/credit special categories' caveat is real but is a US political/social-issue sub-category nuance and is irrelevant to Brazil — do not cite it as if it widened Brazilian coverage. (b) The 'one source claims commercial creative IS exposed via API with limited fields' is partly true but EU-scoped: under DSA the API does return commercial ad CREATIVE/structured fields (ad copy, headlines) for EU-delivered ads while withholding spend/impressions/targeting for non-regulated ads; this limited-field exposure is an EU/DSA phenomenon and does NOT extend to Brazilian commercial ads, so it does not weaken the claim for Brazil. Practical alternative paths (not the /ads_archive API): the Meta Content Library / Content Library API exists for richer commercial data but is gated behind researcher/academic approval via CASD vetting (not usable by a venue for unattended competitive intel), leaving the public Ad Library WEB UI (scrape, against Meta ToS) or paid third-party tools as the only routes to Brazilian competitors' commercial creatives.
verification.evidence: Multiple independent 2025-2026 sources confirm the API scope. (1) Meta's own Transparency Center states the Ad Library API "allows for a deeper analysis of ads about social issues, elections or politics, as well as ads that deliver to the EU and associated territories" — no mention of general commercial ads outside the EU. (2) Independent guides state plainly: "Commercial ads are visible in the browser UI but are not returned by the API even with ad_type=ALL... query the same page_id through the API, and get an empty result set — not a bug... ad_type=ALL means 'all within the political+issue+special-categories scope.'" (3) On geography: "The API only works for ads running in the European Union, Brazil (limited scope), or if they are tagged as political/social cause ads... for most countries outside the UK/EU, non-political ads aren't archived" — Brazil's non-political-tagged limited scope = political/issue ads delivered in Brazil, not commercial. (4) Primores guide confirms the EU/DSA limited-field nuance: for commercial (non-regulated) ads "you won't get impressions, spend, or targeting data... structured fields like ad copy and headlines remain available," with "weaker coverage: non-EU commercial ads" and "non-EU commercial ad retrieval incomplete compared to the web UI." (5) An explicit 2026 statement: "as of 2026, the Meta Ad Library API only provides access to political and social issue ads running in European Union countries, with no access to commercial/brand advertising data via the API." (6) Brazil legal scan: PL 2630 (Fake News Bill, the closest DSA analogue) inactive since late 2024 with no timeline to resume; Brazil's 2025 digital law (ECA Digital / Lei 15.211/2025) governs minors online, inspired by but not equivalent to the DSA's ad-transparency regime — so no Brazilian law forces commercial-ad transparency. (7) Richer commercial data path = Meta Content Library / Content Library API, gated through researcher approval (CASD vetting), not an unattended-automation route. No counter-evidence found that the /ads_archive API returns Brazilian commercial ads; the only 'commercial creative via API' evidence is EU/DSA-scoped and thus does not apply to Brazil.

keyFindings.claim: Apify's Facebook Ad Library Scraper actors are the most pragmatic way to pull a NAMED competitor's paid ads daily from Brazil: input a Page URL/ID, filter country=BR, get creatives/copy/CTAs/start-dates, ~$0.75 per 1,000 ads, full REST API + scheduling, no Meta app review needed.
keyFindings.evidence: Apify actor docs: 'scrape ads run by given list of facebook pages', accepts 2-letter ISO country codes (BR), pay-per-event '$0.75 / 1,000 ads', 'No Facebook login, Meta app review, API token, or Ad Library API approval required', and the Apify REST API lets you 'manage, schedule, and run Actors'. It scrapes the Ad Library WEB UI, so it captures ordinary commercial ads the official API omits.
keyFindings.implication: This is the core building block of the unattended routine: a daily Apify schedule (or your own cron calling the Apify run API) per competitor Page, output to dataset/webhook. Cost is trivial for a handful of local competitors. Fragility risk: it depends on Meta's UI not changing and on Meta tolerating scraping (against Meta TOS), so pick a maintained actor and expect occasional breakage.

verification.claim: Apify's Facebook Ad Library Scraper actors are the most pragmatic way to pull a NAMED competitor's paid ads daily from Brazil: input a Page URL/ID, filter country=BR, get creatives/copy/CTAs/start-dates, ~$0.75 per 1,000 ads, full REST API + scheduling, no Meta app review needed.
verification.correctedClaim: Broadly correct and the strategic core is sound, but imprecise on three points. CORRECT: For a named competitor's ordinary commercial ads, scraping the public Ad Library web UI is genuinely the only viable programmatic path, because Meta's OFFICIAL Ad Library API does NOT return commercial ads at all — as of 2025-2026 it is restricted to political/social-issue ads and is effectively EU-only (further curtailed when Meta withdrew EU political ads in Oct 2025 over the TTPA). A specific Apify actor (curious_coder/facebook-ads-library-scraper) does price at $0.75 / 1,000 ads (pay-per-event), accepts a Facebook Page URL or ad-library search URL plus a 2-letter ISO country code (BR is valid), returns ~57 fields including creatives, ad copy, CTAs and run/start dates, and states verbatim that NO Facebook login, Meta app review, API token, or Ad Library API approval is required. Apify also provides a full REST API (run-actor / run-sync endpoints) AND a native Schedules feature (cron / @daily / timezone), so unattended daily runs are real. IMPRECISIONS TO FIX: (1) There is no single 'Apify Facebook Ad Library Scraper' — it is a marketplace of many competing actors ($0.49 to $10 per 1,000 ads; Apify's own official actor is ~$5/1,000). The $0.75 figure is specific to the curious_coder actor, not 'Apify's' generically; name the actor. (2) Scheduling lives at the Apify PLATFORM level (Schedules + REST), not in that actor's own docs — true, but worth stating precisely. (3) 'No Meta app review' is true but omits the real costs: this is unauthorized scraping under Meta's Platform Terms — legally defensible for logged-off public data after Meta v. Bright Data (2024), but still ToS-prohibited, IP-blockable, CAPTCHA-able, and these third-party actors are FRAGILE (they break when Meta changes the UI/GraphQL and can silently return partial data), so an unattended daily routine needs breakage/empty-result monitoring, not set-and-forget. (4) Scope caveat: for commercial (non-political) ads in Brazil you get creative + copy + start date only — spend, impressions and reach are NOT available for commercial ads even via scraping, so don't expect spend data. Net: 'most pragmatic LOW-COST option for a named competitor, provided you pin a specific actor, schedule via Apify Platform, and monitor for breakage and rate-limited partial results.'
verification.evidence: curious_coder actor page confirms '$0.75 / 1,000 ads', accepts Page URLs and ad-library search URLs, a 2-letter ISO country code (ISO 3166-1 alpha-2), and states 'No Facebook login, Meta app review, or API token needed'; its /api page confirms run / run-sync / run-sync-get-dataset-items REST endpoints (token required). Apify docs confirm a Schedules feature with cron, @daily shortcuts and per-schedule timezones, plus REST endpoints to create/update schedules. Multiple 2026 sources confirm the official Meta Ad Library API returns only political/social-issue ads (EU-focused) and NOT commercial ads, and that spend/impressions are withheld for commercial ads even in the web UI/scrapers (Brazil included). admakeai.com documents that Ad Library scrapers break on Meta UI/GraphQL changes and can silently return partial data on rate limits; Meta v. Bright Data (Jan 2024) held that logged-off scraping of public data is not governed by Meta's account ToS, while Meta's Platform Terms still prohibit automated collection. Search also surfaced 10+ distinct competing Apify actors at prices from $0.49 to $10 per 1,000 ads, refuting the singular 'Apify's actor' framing.

keyFindings.claim: For the ORGANIC side (a named competitor's Instagram/TikTok posts), Apify's Instagram Profile Scraper and TikTok Scraper are far cheaper and better-fit than social-listening suites: ~$0.50–1.00 per 1,000 IG posts, ~$0.30/1k TikTok posts, REST API + scheduling.
keyFindings.evidence: Apify: Instagram Scraper 'from $0.005/query + $0.0005/post' (~$0.50/1,000 posts) scraping profiles/reels; TikTok Scraper '$0.30 per 1K posts' with pay-per-event; both run via the schedulable Apify REST API.
keyFindings.implication: Use a per-account scraper (profile → latest posts) for organic monitoring rather than a keyword-mention listening tool. You get the competitor's actual posts/captions/engagement directly, which is what feeds a discovery process. Same TOS/fragility caveat as the ad scraper.

keyFindings.claim: Foreplay Spyder is the best purpose-built named-competitor ad tracker, but its API is only in beta on higher tiers — it is the strongest 'buy, don't build' option if a per-seat SaaS is acceptable.
keyFindings.evidence: Foreplay Spyder 'automatically tracks selected brands and surfaces new ads as they launch', 'tracks every ad they launch on all Meta advertising platforms 24/7 while keeping a complete historical record', with automated competitor reports/alerts. Pricing ~$49/mo (Inspiration) and ~$99/mo (Full Workflow); 'API beta is only available on Workflow and Agency plans as of February 2026.' Covers Facebook/Instagram/TikTok.
keyFindings.implication: If the founder prefers a managed UI with alerting over maintaining scrapers, Foreplay Spyder is the pick — but because the API is beta-only, a fully UNATTENDED data pull is not guaranteed yet; you may get dashboards/alerts/exports rather than a stable programmatic feed. Validate the API beta covers Page-level pull before committing. Brazilian local pages are tracked because Spyder pulls from the live Meta Ad Library, not a pre-built index.

keyFindings.claim: Dropshipping/ecommerce ad-spy SaaS (AdSpy, BigSpy, Minea, PiPiADS) are the WRONG tool here: they are index-based (only ads they happened to crawl), tuned to high-volume ecommerce advertisers, and a small Brazilian local wedding venue is unlikely to be in their database; they also largely lack an automation API.
keyFindings.evidence: AdSpy ~$149/mo, BigSpy from ~$9/mo, Minea ~$49/mo, PiPiADS ~$77/mo. Sources describe them as 'paid scraper SaaS' with 'no API access' and note their databases skew to ecommerce/dropshipping; Minea is 'best for dropshippers, eCommerce sellers'. Whether a given named local advertiser is indexed is not guaranteed — coverage is what their crawlers caught, not on-demand by Page.
keyFindings.implication: Don't anchor the routine on these. Even if you subscribe, a named local venue may simply not appear, and without an API you can't automate the pull. At best they're a manual, occasional research supplement, not the spine of a daily unattended job.

keyFindings.claim: Social-listening suites (Brandwatch, Sprout Social, Brand24, Mention) are keyword/mention-oriented and expensive; they overshoot 'pull this one named account's own posts daily' and most gate API access behind enterprise tiers.
keyFindings.evidence: Pricing: Brand24/Mention ~$41–$249/mo, Sprout Social $99–$399/user/mo plus a Listening module at ~$2,000–$8,000/yr, Brandwatch enterprise ~$800–$5,000+/mo custom. These track mentions of keywords/brands across many sources rather than reliably exporting a specific account's own post feed; API access is typically enterprise-tier.
keyFindings.implication: Only worth it if the founder later wants broad market/sentiment listening (what people say ABOUT competitors and the category) rather than competitor-owned content. For the stated 'monitor a named competitor's posts/ads' job they are overkill and overpriced vs. Apify. Portuguese/pt-BR is generally supported by the bigger suites, but confirm per vendor.

keyFindings.claim: AdClarity (Semrush) and SocialPeta cover Latin America / global creatives and AdClarity has an API, but neither is a clean fit for tracking a specific small local advertiser daily.
keyFindings.evidence: AdClarity tiers ~$129/mo (Starter, 3mo history) and ~$298/mo (Professional, 6mo history), +$69/market/mo, covers 51 markets incl. Latin America and 'has an API'. SocialPeta indexes 1.3B+ creatives across 70 channels. Both are panel/index-based ad-intelligence, strong for big brands/display but uncertain coverage of one small Brazilian wedding venue.
keyFindings.implication: AdClarity's API is the only one in the dropshipping/intelligence cluster that's genuinely automatable, but its panel model means a tiny local advertiser may have thin or no data, and per-market fees add up. Treat as a possible add-on for broader display/competitor-strategy context, not the named-page daily feed.

result.bottomLine: FEASIBLE to build a daily unattended routine, but only via web-UI scraping — not via an official competitor-data API. Recommended stack (most pragmatic, in order): (1) Apify Facebook Ad Library Scraper + Apify Instagram/TikTok Scraper, driven by the Apify REST API on a daily schedule, one run per named competitor Page, country=BR — handles BOTH paid ads and organic, ~tens of US$/month for a handful of competitors, JSON/webhook output that feeds your discovery process directly. This is the spine. (2) Foreplay Spyder as the turnkey alternative/supplement (~$99/mo Workflow tier) if you'd rather have a managed UI with auto-alerts and a complete historical ad record per brand — but confirm its beta API actually exposes a programmatic per-Page pull before relying on it for full automation. (3) Optionally layer a real social-listening tool (Brand24 ~$199–$249/mo) LATER if the goal expands from 'competitor-owned content' to 'category/sentiment chatter ABOUT competitors.' Avoid AdSpy/BigSpy/Minea/PiPiADS for this — index-based, ecommerce-skewed, no automation API, likely no coverage of a small local venue. Hard caveat: all scraping routes ride on Meta/Instagram/TikTok public UIs against their TOS and break when the UI changes — design the routine to fail loudly (alert on empty/zero-result runs) and budget maintenance, since 'unattended' here means 'monitored unattended,' not 'set and forget forever.'
result.brazilCaveats:
Brazil has no EU-style DSA all-ads transparency law, so there is NO regulatory mandate forcing a complete public ad archive — outside the EU the official Meta Ad Library API archives only political/social-issue ads, leaving commercial wedding-venue ads accessible only through the public Ad Library WEB UI (hence scraping is required, not optional). Brazilian ad transparency is described in 2026 research as 'limited and fragmented,' with the Meta Ad Library UI the most reliable single source and TikTok/X/Spotify offering no Brazilian ad repository. The dropshipping ad-spy databases (AdSpy/BigSpy/Minea) claim broad country coverage but their indexes are ecommerce-skewed and unlikely to contain a specific small local pt-BR wedding venue. pt-BR language coverage in the big social-listening suites (Brandwatch/Sprout/Brand24) is generally fine but should be confirmed per vendor. Separately, Brazil's LGPD applies to processing personal data from scraped content — keep the routine to competitor business/marketing content and avoid harvesting third-party personal data, consistent with the repo's LGPD-deferred posture.</parameter>
