result.dimension: Legal / Terms-of-Service / risk of automated scraping
result.summary: For an unattended daily routine, the honest legal picture is: scraping public ad-library / public social pages is not a crime and almost certainly not a CFAA-style violation when done logged-out (the US case law — hiQ v. LinkedIn and Meta v. Bright Data — points strongly that way), but it does breach the platforms' Terms of Service, and ToS-as-contract is the real (and only meaningful) civil exposure. That exposure is realistically near-zero for a single small Brazilian wedding venue pulling a handful of competitor pages once a day: platforms enforce technically (rate-limits, IP blocks, CAPTCHAs, account bans) far more than legally, and lawsuits/cease-and-desist letters target high-volume commercial scrapers and resellers, not low-volume internal market research. The two things that actually deserve respect are (a) stay logged-out and low-and-slow to keep enforcement squarely in "technical annoyance" territory rather than "breach of an agreement you accepted," and (b) LGPD: scraping is "tratamento de dados" under Brazilian law, the ANPD has made raspagem a 2025 enforcement priority and already issued its first fine, so the routine must store ad copy/creatives (fine) while minimizing and justifying any real-person personal data captured in those creatives. Copyright is a non-issue for internal analysis but becomes one the instant you reuse a competitor's creative in your own ads.

keyFindings.claim: Scraping PUBLIC ad-library / public pages while logged-OUT is not governed by Meta's Terms of Service — a US federal court ruled exactly this and Meta abandoned the case.
keyFindings.evidence: Meta Platforms v. Bright Data (N.D. Cal., Judge Edward Chen, summary judgment Jan 23 2024): Meta's ToS 'do not apply to and prohibit Bright Data's scraping of publicly available data while logged off' because a logged-out scraper is not a 'user' bound by the contract. Meta dropped the suit ~1 month later (Feb 2024). Called the 'most important legal opinion related to web scraping to date.'
keyFindings.implication: Keep the daily routine LOGGED OUT against public ad libraries / public pages. Never use venue Meta/Google/TikTok logins to harvest competitor data — logging in is what flips you from 'not a party to the contract' into 'a user breaching the ToS.' This single design choice removes most of the contract exposure.

verification.claim: Scraping PUBLIC ad-library / public pages while logged-OUT is not governed by Meta's Terms of Service — a US federal court ruled exactly this and Meta abandoned the case. (Cited: Meta v. Bright Data, N.D. Cal., Judge Chen, summary judgment Jan 23 2024; Meta dropped the suit ~Feb 2024; "most important legal opinion related to web scraping to date.")
verification.correctedClaim: CORE IS TRUE, but imprecise on three points. PRECISE VERSION: In Meta Platforms, Inc. v. Bright Data Ltd. (No. 3:23-cv-00077-EMC, N.D. Cal., Judge Edward M. Chen, summary judgment Jan 23, 2024), the court held that Meta's Facebook/Instagram Terms — properly construed — do NOT PROHIBIT scraping of publicly available data performed while LOGGED OFF, because a logged-off scraper is not a "user" bound by the contract (the Terms apply to account holders while logged in). It also held Meta's "survival" clause could not bind Bright Data in perpetuity after account termination. Meta then dismissed its remaining tortious-interference claim without prejudice (~Feb 23, 2024) and waived appeal — effectively conceding. THREE CORRECTIONS: (1) "not governed by ToS" overstates it — the court interpreted the ToS and ruled they do not BAR logged-off public scraping; that is a contract-interpretation holding, not a statement that no terms apply. (2) The ruling decided only the BREACH-OF-CONTRACT (ToS) theory. It did NOT decide copyright, CFAA, trespass, misappropriation, or privacy/data-protection law — those remain separate, live risks, so ToS-clearance does not make scraping lawful across the board. (3) It is a DISTRICT-court summary judgment (persuasive, not binding precedent); there was no appellate ruling, and Meta signaled it could rewrite its Terms. The "most important legal opinion to date" label is commentator framing (e.g., Eric Goldman's blog called it an "earthquake"; some reserve "most important" for hiQ v. LinkedIn), not language from the court. Also note logged-IN scraping remains ToS-prohibited, and — critical for a Brazil-based operator — Brazil's LGPD still applies to publicly available PERSONAL data per ANPD regardless of this US ruling.
verification.evidence: Confirmed via primary-source legal analyses. Farella Braun + Martel and Quinn Emanuel client alerts confirm: Judge Chen, N.D. Cal., granted Bright Data summary judgment on Meta's breach-of-contract claim Jan 23 2024, holding the Terms apply only to "users" (account holders) while logged in and do not prohibit logged-off public scraping; the survival clause was unenforceable. Quinn Emanuel explicitly lists the caveats: scope is limited to Meta's terms (not binding precedent for other sites); the decision does NOT address logged-in scraping (still prohibited); and other doctrines — copyright, CFAA, privacy law — remain unaddressed. PRNewswire (Feb 23 2024) confirms Meta filed to dismiss the remaining tortious-interference claim without prejudice and waived appeal — not a settlement, no concession demanded of Bright Data. Eric Goldman's Technology & Marketing Law Blog confirms it is a district-court ruling and that other anti-scraping doctrines (CFAA, etc.) could still apply ("a loss on any of the legal doctrines would still be a total loss for Bright Data"); it characterizes the decision as an "earthquake," not as the single "most important legal opinion to date" (that superlative is applied to hiQ v. LinkedIn). The 2024-2025 X Corp. v. Bright Data case (Judge Alsup) reinforces the public-data direction (X's contract/tort claims tossed, copyright preemption applied, Bright Data antitrust counterclaims survived, settled in principle 2025). Brazil ANPD / ICLG / DLA Piper sources confirm LGPD applies to publicly available personal data, so the US ToS holding does not clear LGPD exposure for a São Paulo operator.

keyFindings.claim: Scraping public data does NOT violate the CFAA (anti-hacking statute), but you can still LOSE on breach-of-contract for violating ToS — hiQ paid $500k and shut down.
keyFindings.evidence: hiQ v. LinkedIn: 9th Circuit (2019, reaffirmed Apr 2022) held the CFAA does not reach automated collection of publicly accessible data. But the Dec 2022 settlement: LinkedIn WON on breach-of-contract and trespass, hiQ paid $500,000, accepted a permanent injunction to stop scraping and delete code/data, and the company shut down. Distinction: logged-out public scraping (Bright Data) vs hiQ which had accepted LinkedIn's user agreement.
keyFindings.implication: The risk is civil/contractual, not criminal. For a low-volume internal routine the practical odds of a contract suit are negligible — these actions target commercial-scale scrapers/resellers — but the lesson is concrete: don't accept and then breach a clickwrap/login agreement, and never resell or publicly republish the harvested data (selling/licensing scraped data is the conduct that draws suits).

verification.claim: Scraping public data does NOT violate the CFAA, but you can still LOSE on breach-of-contract for violating ToS — hiQ paid $500k and shut down.
verification.correctedClaim: Broadly correct, with one important nuance. The 9th Circuit (hiQ v. LinkedIn, 2019; reaffirmed Apr 2022) held the CFAA's "without authorization" prong does NOT reach automated collection of data that is publicly accessible and not behind an authentication gate — so logged-out public scraping is not "hacking" under the CFAA. The live legal risk is breach of CONTRACT: on Nov 4, 2022 the N.D. Cal. granted summary judgment that hiQ breached LinkedIn's User Agreement (its anti-scraping and fake-profile terms), because hiQ had accepted that agreement when it created a corporate account. The Dec 2022 resolution was a STIPULATED/CONSENT judgment (agreed, non-precedential): $500,000 against hiQ plus a permanent injunction to stop scraping and destroy all source code, data, and algorithms; hiQ is now defunct. IMPRECISION TO FIX: the stipulation did NOT leave CFAA entirely off the table — it let LinkedIn establish CFAA liability too, but that CFAA piece was tied specifically to "hiQ's direct access to password-protected pages on LinkedIn's platforms using fake accounts," i.e. auth-gated access, NOT to the public scraping. So the public-scraping CFAA safe harbor stands; hiQ's CFAA exposure came from logging in with fake accounts. The $500k bundled breach of contract + CFAA + California Penal Code 502 + trespass to chattels + misappropriation + spoliation sanctions, not breach-of-contract alone. The logged-out vs. logged-in/accepted-ToS distinction the claim draws is correct and was reinforced afterward: Meta v. Bright Data (Jan 23, 2024, Judge Chen, N.D. Cal.) held Facebook/Instagram Terms do not bar logged-OFF scraping of public data, and X Corp v. Bright Data had its scraping claims dismissed (May 2024, Copyright Act preemption) and then settled confidentially in mid-2025 with Bright Data continuing to operate. Practical takeaway for unattended competitor-ad/social monitoring in 2026: the loss vector is CONTRACT (triggered by accepting a platform's ToS, typically via having/using an account) and auth-gated access — not public scraping per se. Scraping while logged out, without an account, of genuinely public pages is the defensible posture; logging into an account whose ToS bans scraping (or using fake accounts to reach gated content) is where liability and CFAA exposure attach.
verification.evidence: 9th Circuit hiQ v. LinkedIn (2019, reaffirmed Apr 18 2022): CFAA "without authorization" does not apply to "websites that generally permitted public access to their data and took no steps to demarcate their data as private or require the use of an authorization system." Nov 4 2022 N.D. Cal. summary judgment: hiQ breached LinkedIn's User Agreement via (1) automated scraping of profiles and (2) hiring crowdsourced "turkers" to create fake profiles to access the platform; hiQ "expressly agreed to the user agreement when it created its corporate account." Dec 7-8 2022 stipulated consent judgment: "$500,000 judgment to be entered against hiQ for (1) a breach of contract based on LinkedIn's user agreement; (2) a violation of the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) 'based on hiQ's data collection practices and based on hiQ's direct access to password-protected pages on LinkedIn's platforms using fake accounts;' (3) a violation of California's law prohibiting unauthorized computer access; (4) violations of the common law torts of trespass to chattels and misappropriation; and (5) sanctions awarded to LinkedIn based on hiQ's spoliation," plus a permanent injunction to "cease all data scraping on LinkedIn's websites and destroy all source code, data, and algorithms." Commentators note the CFAA stipulation is "an agreement between the parties" and does "not serve as precedent." Meta v. Bright Data (Jan 23 2024): "The Facebook and Instagram Terms do not bar logged-off scraping of public data; perforce it does not prohibit the sale of such public data" — terms bind only an actively logged-in user. X Corp v. Bright Data: dismissed May 10 2024 (failed to allege a claim on access to its public site; copying public data preempted by Copyright Act); settled confidentially mid-2025, Bright Data continuing to operate.

keyFindings.claim: Every platform's ToS formally prohibits automated scraping — Meta, Google, TikTok and Pinterest all bar it without express written permission.
keyFindings.evidence: Meta Automated Data Collection Terms: 'You will not engage in Automated Data Collection without first obtaining Meta's express written permission' and 'acceptance of these Terms alone does not constitute the required written permission.' Google ToS bars automated access exceeding what a human can produce. Pinterest ToS: 'You agree not to use any robot, spider, crawler, scraper... to access the Services or to extract data,' and even its API dev guidelines forbid data extraction. TikTok developer terms prohibit scraping.
keyFindings.implication: There is no platform whose ToS lets you scrape — so 'is it allowed?' is always 'no.' The realistic question is enforcement, not permission. Treat ToS prohibition as a given and manage it by staying logged-out, low-volume, and internal-use-only rather than by trying to find a compliant scraping path (there isn't one).

keyFindings.claim: There is NO official API exposing competitors' COMMERCIAL ad data usable from Brazil — the official ad-library APIs are political/social-issue-only or EU-only.
keyFindings.evidence: Meta Ad Library API returns only political & social-issue ads (commercial brand ads are visible in the browser UI but do not come back from /ads_archive, and no spend is exposed). TikTok Commercial Content Library API: 'only data from Europe is available' (built for EU DSA Art. 39), and access is denied to commercial entities, agencies and marketers. Google Ads Transparency Center has no official public API. So competitor wedding-venue commercial ads are reachable web-UI-only.
keyFindings.implication: The routine cannot be built on a clean official API for the data that matters (competitors' commercial creatives/copy). Either scrape the public web UIs (the ToS-but-not-CFAA path above) or pay a third-party data vendor (Apify actors, SerpApi, BrightData, ad-intel SaaS) that absorbs the scraping infrastructure and some of the ToS exposure. The 'API where possible' goal mostly fails for competitor commercial data in Brazil.

keyFindings.claim: Under Brazilian LGPD, scraping is regulated 'tratamento de dados' and the ANPD has made raspagem an active enforcement priority — public availability does NOT mean free to reuse.
keyFindings.evidence: ANPD Radar Tecnológico nº3 (Nov 2024) formalizes that web scraping is data processing subject to the full LGPD. Raspagem entered ANPD's 2025 regulatory agenda with a commitment to at least three enforcement actions, and the ANPD already applied its first sanction against a telemarketing company that scraped publicly available data without a valid basis. Public personal data may only be reused for the original purpose, or for a new purpose under a valid Art. 7 basis such as legítimo interesse (which requires a balancing/LIA and reasonable-expectation test).
keyFindings.implication: Storing competitors' ad COPY and CREATIVES (the marketing artifact itself) is largely fine — it is corporate/marketing content, not personal data. The LGPD trigger is incidental personal data inside creatives: identifiable faces, real customer names/testimonials, a sole-proprietor competitor's personal identifiers. Mitigate by storing creative + copy + metadata, minimizing/redacting incidental personal data, documenting 'legítimo interesse' (competitive market intelligence) with a short LIA, keeping it strictly internal, and setting retention limits. Do not build a database keyed on real individuals.

keyFindings.claim: Real-world enforcement for low-volume daily scraping is overwhelmingly TECHNICAL (IP blocks, CAPTCHAs, bans), not legal — lawsuits target high-volume commercial scrapers.
keyFindings.evidence: Industry/legal analyses converge: violating ToS 'isn't automatically illegal' but invites technical enforcement (IP block, account suspension) and, for scale players, contract disputes. Platforms reserve legal action for high-volume extraction and for parties who resell/republish data. Scraping public business/marketing data is consistently described as lower-risk than scraping private personal data. The named lawsuits (hiQ, Bright Data, and others) are all against commercial-scale scraping operations, not small internal users.
keyFindings.implication: For one venue pulling a small set of competitor pages once per day, expect at worst occasional IP blocks / CAPTCHA challenges — an engineering problem (rotate UA respectfully, throttle, cache, back off), not a legal one. Budget for graceful degradation when a source blocks you, and never escalate volume to the point you look like a commercial scraper. The pragmatic posture: proceed, logged-out, low-and-slow, internal-only.

keyFindings.claim: Copyright is a non-issue for internal analysis of competitor creatives, but becomes a real issue the moment you reuse a creative in your own ads.
keyFindings.evidence: Competitive-intelligence practice routinely stores competitors' ad creatives, copy, CTAs and images for analysis; this is treated as legitimate use of publicly available data. The line is reuse: 'legally there's no such thing as copying a competitor ad' as a free pass — building your own original creatives is fine, lifting theirs (or their trademarked names) is infringement.
keyFindings.implication: Safe to store and analyze creatives/copy as a private intelligence corpus to inform VVF's own original work (tone, themes, offers). The routine must stay a read/store/analyze pipeline — it must never feed competitor assets directly into VVF-published material. This also keeps the brand-voice invariants intact (VVF copy must be original and on-brand, not derivative).

result.brazilCaveats: Brazil-specific reality is the decisive constraint, in two directions. (1) Coverage: the official ad-library APIs that would let you avoid scraping entirely are EU-centric — Meta's API is political/social-only outside the EU, TikTok's Commercial Content Library is EU-only and closed to marketers, and Google has no official ads-transparency API. So for Brazilian competitor commercial ads the only paths are web-UI scraping or a paid third-party vendor; there is no clean official-API route. (2) LGPD/ANPD: scraping is squarely 'tratamento de dados' under LGPD, ANPD formalized this in Radar Tecnológico nº3 (Nov 2024), put raspagem on its 2025 enforcement agenda (≥3 actions), and has already issued its first scraping-related fine. 'Dado público' is NOT a free pass — reuse needs a valid Art. 7 basis. For this use case the workable basis is legítimo interesse for internal competitive intelligence, documented with a short LIA, with aggressive minimization of any incidental personal data (faces, named individuals, testimonials) in stored creatives, internal-only use, and a retention limit. Note the US case law (hiQ, Bright Data) is persuasive on the contract/CFAA question but has zero authority over LGPD — a Brazilian business is governed by LGPD regardless of how favorable the US scraping precedents look.
result.bottomLine: Feasible, with a clear-eyed posture: build the daily routine, but design it to keep risk in the 'technical annoyance' band rather than the 'breach of contract / LGPD violation' band. Concretely: (1) scrape competitor commercial ads from PUBLIC web UIs (Meta Ad Library, Google Ads Transparency Center, public TikTok/Pinterest/Instagram pages) LOGGED OUT — never with the venue's own platform logins — because logged-out public scraping is what the Bright Data ruling put outside Meta's ToS and outside the CFAA per hiQ. (2) Accept that every platform's ToS still formally forbids scraping; this is contract-grade, not criminal-grade, exposure and is realistically immaterial at low volume — enforcement at this scale is IP blocks/CAPTCHAs, which is an engineering problem. (3) Run low-and-slow (once daily, modest page counts, throttled, cached, graceful back-off) so you never resemble a commercial-scale scraper, which is who actually gets sued. (4) Use the official Meta Ad Library API only where it helps (it won't cover commercial ads outside the EU) and consider a paid third-party vendor (Apify/SerpApi/ad-intel SaaS) to offload scraping infrastructure and ToS exposure if reliability matters. (5) For LGPD, store ad copy + creatives + metadata for internal CI under documented legítimo interesse with a short LIA, minimize/redact incidental personal data (faces, named people, testimonials), keep it internal-only with a retention limit — and never resell, republish, or reuse competitors' creatives in VVF's own ads (that flips copyright + brand-voice invariants). Loop in counsel only to bless the LIA template once; the rest is process discipline. This is a green-light with guardrails, not a stop.

result.dimension: Claude Code routines and Agent SDK for daily competitor-ad monitoring

keyFindings.claim: Routines run headless in Anthropic cloud and survive laptop closed; local scheduled tasks do not.
keyFindings.evidence: Claude Code Routines (v2.1.105+, April 2026, research preview, beta header experimental-cc-routine-2026-04-01) run on Anthropic-managed VMs and keep working with the Mac closed. Desktop scheduled tasks fire only while the app is open; CLI loop and the in-session Cron tools are session-scoped and die when the terminal closes.
keyFindings.implication: An unattended daily routine must use cloud Routines (Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise plus Claude Code on the web) or headless claude -p on GitHub Actions cron or your admin/api-server host. Do not rely on the desktop scheduler or in-session cron.

verification.claim: Routines run headless in Anthropic cloud and survive laptop closed; local scheduled tasks do not. (Evidence: Claude Code Routines v2.1.105+, April 2026, research preview, beta header experimental-cc-routine-2026-04-01, run on Anthropic-managed VMs and keep working with the Mac closed; Desktop scheduled tasks fire only while the app is open; CLI /loop and in-session Cron tools are session-scoped and die when the terminal closes.)
verification.correctedClaim: CONFIRMED by Anthropic's official Claude Code docs (current as of June 2026), with one precision refinement. (1) Routines DO run headless/unattended on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure and survive a closed laptop: the routines doc states verbatim "Routines execute on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure, so they keep working when your laptop is closed," and they "run autonomously as full Claude Code cloud sessions: there is no permission-mode picker and no approval prompts during a run." (2) Version/preview/header details are all correct: Routines shipped in Week 16 (April 13-17, 2026, v2.1.105-v2.1.113), are flagged "in research preview," and the /fire API endpoint ships under the exact beta header anthropic-beta: experimental-cc-routine-2026-04-01. (3) Both local mechanisms indeed do NOT survive a closed laptop, but they are TWO distinct things with different reasons -- the claim is right to say "local does not," just imprecise to lump them: (a) Desktop scheduled tasks (the "Local" routine type) run on your machine and, per the official Desktop doc, "only fire while the app is open and your computer is awake ... Closing the laptop lid still puts it to sleep" -- note it is the APP that must be running, not a manual chat session (the comparison table lists Desktop as "Requires open session: No"); (b) the CLI /loop command and in-session Cron tools (CronCreate/CronList/CronDelete) are session-scoped: "Tasks only fire while Claude Code is running and idle. Closing the terminal or letting the session exit stops them firing," and a fresh conversation clears them (a 7-day expiry also applies). No counter-evidence, recent reversal, or regional exception was found; the only caveats are access-level (Routines require a Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise plan with Claude Code on the web enabled, are billed against subscription usage with a daily run cap, and can be disabled org-wide by Team/Enterprise admins) -- these gate availability, not the run-location behavior the claim asserts. For the VVF competitor-monitoring use case, a cloud Routine is the correct mechanism for an unattended daily run that survives the laptop being closed; minimum schedule interval is 1 hour, and a cloud Routine has NO access to local files (fresh repo clone each run) and reaches external sites only through its environment's network allowlist or configured connectors.
verification.evidence: Official Anthropic Claude Code documentation, verified directly: (1) Routines doc (code.claude.com/docs/en/routines): "Routines execute on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure, so they keep working when your laptop is closed"; "Routines run autonomously as full Claude Code cloud sessions: there is no permission-mode picker and no approval prompts during a run"; note "Routines are in research preview"; /fire curl example includes header "anthropic-beta: experimental-cc-routine-2026-04-01" and a Warning block referencing the same dated beta header. (2) Desktop scheduled tasks doc (code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-scheduled-tasks): "A local task runs on your machine ... but only fires while the app is open and your computer is awake. A remote routine runs on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure even when your computer is off"; "Tasks only run while the desktop app is running and your computer is awake ... Closing the laptop lid still puts it to sleep." Comparison table: Desktop "Requires machine on: Yes," "Requires open session: No." (3) Scheduled-tasks/loop doc (code.claude.com/docs/en/scheduled-tasks): comparison table marks /loop "Requires open session: Yes," "Requires machine on: Yes"; Limitations: "Tasks only fire while Claude Code is running and idle. Closing the terminal or letting the session exit stops them firing"; tools listed are CronCreate/CronList/CronDelete; 7-day expiry noted. (4) What's-new digest (code.claude.com/docs/en/whats-new): Week 16, April 13-17 2026, tags v2.1.105-v2.1.113: "Routines on Claude Code on the web fire templated cloud agents from a schedule, GitHub event, or API call." GitHub release v2.1.105 dated April 13, 2026. Secondary sources (MindStudio, DataCamp, winbuzzer, wmedia, junia.ai) all corroborate; no counter-evidence found.

keyFindings.claim: WebFetch officially does not execute JavaScript and cannot retrieve SPA-rendered content.
keyFindings.evidence: Anthropic platform docs verbatim: the web fetch tool currently does not support websites dynamically rendered with JavaScript. It does an HTTP GET, returns text or PDF only, no JS, no cookies, no element interaction.
keyFindings.implication: WebFetch can read static pages, RSS, sitemaps, JSON API endpoints and PDFs, but cannot open the Meta Ad Library UI or any modern SPA. Designing the routine around WebFetch against the UI is a dead end.

verification.claim: WebFetch officially does not execute JavaScript and cannot retrieve SPA-rendered content.
verification.correctedClaim: CONFIRMED, with two precisions. The central assertion is directly stated in Anthropic's official docs, verbatim: "The web fetch tool currently does not support websites dynamically rendered with JavaScript" (platform.claude.com, web-fetch-tool page, current as of June 2026). It performs an HTTP retrieval plus automatic text extraction with NO rendering engine, so a React/Vue SPA returns the initial HTML shell, not the rendered DOM. Content types are restricted to text and PDF only — the documented error code `unsupported_content_type` reads "Content type not supported (only text and PDF)." For a daily unattended competitor-monitoring routine, this means WebFetch is useless against any modern JS-rendered competitor site or social feed (Instagram, Facebook, most Meta Ad Library surfaces, Wix/Squarespace SPA venue sites); you must route those through a headless-browser scraper (Playwright/Puppeteer), a rendering API (Firecrawl/Bright Data/ScrapingBee), or an official data API. PRECISIONS to the stated evidence: (1) The docs do NOT specify the HTTP verb — they say it 'retrieves the full text content from the specified URL'; calling it specifically a 'GET' is a reasonable inference but not documented. (2) 'no cookies, no element interaction, no clicking' are CORRECT by implication (the tool has no API surface for them and only fetches+extracts) but are NOT verbatim-documented limitations — the only explicitly stated limitations are the JavaScript one and the text/PDF-only content restriction. Also note the term 'WebFetch' covers two related surfaces that share this behavior: the Claude Code agent tool and the Anthropic API `web_fetch` server tool (latest version web_fetch_20260318); the official docs page documents the server tool, and both also block authenticated/private URLs.
verification.evidence: Anthropic platform docs (web-fetch-tool), fetched June 2026, state verbatim in a Note: "The web fetch tool currently does not support websites dynamically rendered with JavaScript." The "How web fetch works" section describes only: (1) Claude decides to fetch, (2) the API retrieves full text content from the URL, (3) automatic text extraction for PDFs, (4) Claude analyzes — no rendering/JS-execution step exists. The documented error code list includes `unsupported_content_type`: "Content type not supported (only text and PDF)", confirming text+PDF are the sole outputs (response examples show media_type text/plain or application/pdf). URL validation restricts fetches to URLs already in context (no arbitrary navigation). The Claude Code WebFetch tool schema in-session corroborates: "Fetches a URL, converts the page to markdown" and "Fails on authenticated/private URLs" — consistent with no cookies/auth/JS. Independent third-party tests (Firecrawl, Bright Data, ScrapegraphAI blogs, June 2026 search) corroborate: SPAs/React/Vue pages return an empty shell. The docs page now lives at platform.claude.com (docs.anthropic.com and docs.claude.com 301/302 redirect there) — no 2025-2026 change removed the JS limitation; web_fetch_20260318 added dynamic filtering, cache bypass, and response inclusion, none of which is JS rendering.

keyFindings.claim: The Meta Ad Library is a JS SPA that returns an empty shell to WebFetch, so WebFetch alone extracts no ad data.
keyFindings.evidence: Ad Library data is loaded via JavaScript; scrapers need full JS rendering via a headless browser with Playwright or Puppeteer. SPAs ship minimal HTML and render client-side, so the initial HTML has almost no useful data.
keyFindings.implication: To get competitor ad creatives or copy you need a real rendered browser or the SPA JSON endpoint. A WebFetch-only routine returns nothing useful for this target.

keyFindings.claim: Headless cloud runs lack the Chrome extension and computer-use, but have Bash and a setup script, so a real headless browser can run inside the routine.
keyFindings.evidence: The routine container has a full filesystem, network access, the Claude Code runtime, bash execution and a setup script that runs before the session to install dependencies. No source mentions a Chrome extension or computer-use in headless cloud runs. The sandbox egress and headed-browser policy is thinly documented in the preview.
keyFindings.implication: A pure routine approach can work by installing Playwright in the setup script and scraping or calling the JSON API in Bash, then letting Claude reason over the JSON. Validate egress allowlist, Chromium permission and Meta anti-bot on the cloud IP; if blocked, run Playwright on admin/api-server and use the routine only to trigger and summarize.

keyFindings.claim: The robust pattern is a thin extractor (Playwright or official JSON API) plus Claude as the reasoning layer, not Claude as the scraper.
keyFindings.evidence: For SPAs the clean approach is to call the same JSON API the SPA calls or use Playwright; Claude Code strength is reasoning over structured content, with WebFetch reserved for static trusted pages.
keyFindings.implication: Design the routine as a deterministic extractor producing structured JSON of competitor ads and posts, then Claude reads the JSON, writes the digest, dedupes, flags new creatives and feeds discovery. This isolates the brittle blockable part and fits the repo D-15 data-in-via-adapters split. Keep WebFetch for static slices only.

keyFindings.claim: Routines are a research preview with limits that matter for a daily-forever job.
keyFindings.evidence: Routines are explicitly research preview, gated to paid web-enabled plans. The in-session Cron and loop tools auto-expire after 7 days and are session-only. Cloud durability and quotas beyond the preview are not firmly documented.
keyFindings.implication: Do not build the long-term monitor on in-session cron. Use cloud Routines if you accept preview risk, or build the durable version on GitHub Actions cron or a scheduled job on admin/api-server invoking headless claude -p or the Agent SDK, which removes the preview and plan dependency and gives an unrestricted browser and npm.

result.bottomLine: Feasible, but not as a routine that WebFetches the ad libraries. WebFetch officially cannot render JS or SPAs, and the Meta Ad Library is exactly that, so it returns an empty shell for the primary target. The viable Claude-Code-native path is a cloud Routine (research preview; Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise plus Claude Code on the web; survives laptop closed) whose setup script installs Playwright and runs a headless-browser or JSON-API extractor in Bash, then has Claude synthesize the structured data into a daily market-intel digest. There is no Chrome extension or computer-use in headless cloud runs; Bash plus Playwright is the substitute. The more durable, preview-risk-free alternative is to run the same extractor plus headless claude -p or the Agent SDK on infrastructure you already have (GitHub Actions cron or a scheduled job on admin/api-server), giving an unrestricted browser and npm and avoiding preview and plan gating. Either way the winning architecture is a deterministic extractor feeding Claude as the reasoning layer, not Claude as the scraper.
result.summary: A Claude Code cloud Routine can run daily unattended with the laptop closed, but only on Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise with Claude Code on the web, and Routines is a research preview. The naive design of letting the routine WebFetch the Meta Ad Library does not work: Anthropic docs state WebFetch does not support JavaScript-rendered sites, and the Ad Library and competitor social pages are JS SPAs that return an empty HTML shell. The escape hatch is that a cloud routine is a full Claude Code container with Bash, filesystem, network and a pre-session setup script, so it can install Playwright and drive a real headless browser itself. There is no Chrome extension or computer-use in headless cloud runs; Bash plus Playwright replaces them. The realistic path is a scheduled cloud Routine that runs a Playwright or JSON-API extractor in Bash, then has Claude reason over the extracted JSON. WebFetch is only useful for static endpoints such as RSS, sitemaps, JSON APIs and PDFs, not the SPA UI.
result.brazilCaveats: Nothing in Claude Code itself blocks Brazil use. WebSearch result sourcing is documented as US-only, but that does not block fetching or scraping non-US targets. Routines run on Anthropic US cloud, so the egress IP is US-based, which can trip geo and anti-bot heuristics on Meta properties and is a reason to run the extractor from a Brazil-resident host you control. Plans (Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise) are available in Brazil; billing is USD. The hard Brazil constraints (LGPD on personal data captured from competitor social pages, and whether an official competitor-data API exists) belong to the data-source dimensions, not this capability dimension, which assumes the legality and availability of the underlying data are resolved elsewhere.
